You
You are a contributor preparing a set of changes for upstream submission using the `jj` version control CLI. You have been working in a repository at `/home/user/repo` on a feature but your commits are a bit messy. The repository has a `main` branch and you are working on a bookmark named `feature`. Currently, there are three commits on your bookmark: 1. The first commit adds a new file `utils.py` with some helper functions. 2. The second commit accidentally modifies both `utils.py` (fixing a bug) and adds a new file `api.py` (a new feature) in the same commit. 3. The third commit adds tests for `api.py` in `test_api.py`. Your task is to clean up this history for review: 1. First, you need to `jj split` the second commit so that the bug fix to `utils.py` is in its own commit, and the addition of `api.py` is in another commit. 2. Then, you need to `jj squash` the bug fix commit for `utils.py` into the first commit (the one that originally added `utils.py`). 3. After that, ensure that the `api.py` commit and the `test_api.py` commit remain as separate, subsequent commits on top of the squashed `utils.py` commit. 4. Finally, make sure the `feature` bookmark points to the final commit (`test_api.py`). When you are done, run `jj log -r 'main..feature' -T 'description ++ " "' --no-graph > /home/user/result.txt` to save the final commit descriptions in order (from newest to oldest).

🎉 Task Completed
I have cleaned up the history of the `feature` bookmark in the repository at `/home/user/repo`.

Here's a summary of the actions taken:
1.  **Split the second commit**: I used `jj split -r oxvuysotpyqu utils.py -m "bug fix utils.py"` to separate the bug fix in `utils.py` from the addition of `api.py`. This resulted in two separate commits: one with the bug fix and one with the remaining changes (`api.py`).
2.  **Squashed the bug fix**: I used `jj squash --from oxvuysotpyqu --into lprvnnsluxpw -m "add utils.py"` to merge the bug fix into the original commit that added `utils.py`.
3.  **Renamed the api.py commit**: To ensure a clean history, I renamed the remaining part of the split commit (which originally had the description "mixed changes") to `add api.py` using `jj describe`.
4.  **Verified the order and bookmark**: The resulting history consists of three clean commits:
    - `add utils.py` (including the fix)
    - `add api.py`
    - `add tests` (the original third commit)
    The `feature` bookmark correctly points to the final `add tests` commit.

The final commit descriptions have been saved to `/home/user/result.txt` as requested.

```bash
jj log -r 'main..feature' -T 'description ++ "\n"' --no-graph > /home/user/result.txt
```

The contents of `/home/user/result.txt` are:
```
add tests

add api.py

add utils.py

```
(Note: The double newlines are due to the `jj` description already containing a trailing newline, combined with the `\n` in the template as requested.)

Edit the right side\r\nof the diff until it has the content you want in the first commit. Once you\r\nclose the editor, your revision will be split into two commits.\r\n\r\n[diff editor]:\r\n    https://docs.jj-vcs.dev/latest/config/#editing-diffs\r\n\r\nBy default, the selected changes stay in the original commit, and the\r\nremaining changes go into a new child commit:\r\n\r\n```text\r\nL                 L'\r\n|                 |\r\nK (split)   =>    K\" (remaining)\r\n|                 |\r\nJ                 K' (selected)\r\n                  |\r\n                  J\r\n```\r\n\r\nWith `--parallel/-p`, the two parts become sibling commits instead of\r\nparent and child:\r\n\r\n```text\r\n                  L'\r\nL                / \\\r\n|               K'  |  (selected)\r\nK (split)  =>   |   K\" (remaining)\r\n|                \\ /\r\nJ                 J\r\n```\r\n\r\nWith `-o`, `-A`, or `-B`, the selected changes are extracted into a new\r\ncommit at the specified location, while the remaining changes stay in place:\r\n\r\n```text\r\nM                 M'\r\n|                 |\r\nL                 L'\r\n|                 |\r\nK (split)   =>    K' (remaining, stays here)\r\n|                 |\r\nJ                 J'\r\n                  |\r\n                  K\" (selected, inserted before J with -B J)\r\n```\r\n\r\nIf the change you split had a description, you will be asked to enter a\r\nchange description for each commit. If the change did not have a\r\ndescription, the second commit will not get a description, and you will be\r\nasked for a description only for the first commit.\r\n\r\nSplitting an empty commit is not supported because the same effect can be\r\nachieved with `jj new`.\r\n\r\nUsage: jj split [OPTIONS] [FILESETS]...\r\n\r\nArguments:\r\n  [FILESETS]...\r\n          Files matching any of these filesets are put in the selected changes\r\n\r\nOptions:\r\n  -i, --interactive\r\n          Interactively choose which parts to split\r\n          \r\n          This is the default if no filesets are provided.\r\n\r\n      --tool <NAME>\r\n          Specify diff editor to be used (implies --interactive)\r\n\r\n  -r, --revision <REVSET>\r\n          The revision to split\r\n          \r\n          [default: @]\r\n\r\n  -o, --onto <REVSETS>\r\n          The revision(s) to rebase the selected changes onto (can be repeated to create a merge\r\n          commit)\r\n          \r\n          Extracts the selected changes into a new commit based on the given revision(s). The\r\n          remaining changes stay in the original commit's location.\r\n          \r\n          [aliases: -d, --destination]\r\n\r\n  -A, --insert-after <REVSETS>\r\n          The revision(s) to insert after (can be repeated to create a merge commit)\r\n          \r\n          Extracts the selected changes into a new commit inserted after the given revision(s). The\r\n          remaining changes stay in the original commit's location.\r\n          \r\n          [aliases: --after]\r\n\r\n  -B, --insert-before <REVSETS>\r\n          The revision(s) to insert before (can be repeated to create a merge commit)\r\n          \r\n          Extracts the selected changes into a new commit inserted before the given revision(s). The\r\n          remaining changes stay in the original commit's location.\r\n          \r\n          [aliases: --before]\r\n\r\n  -m, --message <MESSAGE>\r\n          The change description to use (don't open editor)\r\n          \r\n          Sets the description for the first commit (the one containing the selected changes). The\r\n          second commit keeps the original description.\r\n\r\n      --editor\r\n          Open an editor to edit the change description\r\n          \r\n          Forces an editor to open when using `--message` to allow the message to be edited\r\n          afterwards.\r\n\r\n  -p, --parallel\r\n          Split the revision into two parallel revisions instead of a parent and child\r\n\r\n  -h, --help\r\n          Print help (see a summary with '-h')\r\n\r\nGlobal Options:\r\n  -R, --repository <REPOSITORY>\r\n          Path to repository to operate on\r\n          \r\n          By default, Jujutsu searches for the closest .jj/ directory in an ancestor of the current\r\n          working directory.\r\n\r\n      --ignore-working-copy\r\n          Don't snapshot the working copy, and don't update it\r\n          \r\n          By default, Jujutsu snapshots the working copy at the beginning of every command. The\r\n          working copy is also updated at the end of the command, if the command modified the\r\n          working-copy commit (`@`). If you want to avoid snapshotting the working copy and instead\r\n          see a possibly stale working-copy commit, you can use `--ignore-working-copy`. This may be\r\n          useful e.g. in a command prompt, especially if you have another process that commits the\r\n          working copy.\r\n          \r\n          Loading the repository at a specific operation with `--at-operation` implies\r\n          `--ignore-working-copy`.\r\n\r\n      --ignore-immutable\r\n          Allow rewriting immutable commits\r\n          \r\n          By default, Jujutsu prevents rewriting commits in the configured set of immutable commits.\r\n          This option disables that check and lets you rewrite any commit but the root commit.\r\n          \r\n          This option only affects the check. It does not affect the `immutable_heads()` revset or\r\n          the `immutable` template keyword.\r\n\r\n      --at-operation <AT_OPERATION>\r\n          Operation to load the repo at\r\n          \r\n          Operation to load the repo at. By default, Jujutsu loads the repo at the most recent\r\n          operation, or at the merge of the divergent operations if any.\r\n          \r\n          You can use `--at-op=<operation ID>` to see what the repo looked like at an earlier\r\n          operation. For example `jj --at-op=<operation ID> st` will show you what `jj st` would\r\n          have shown you when the given operation had just finished. `--at-op=@` is pretty much the\r\n          same as the default except that divergent operations will never be merged.\r\n          \r\n          Use `jj op log` to find the operation ID you want. Any unambiguous prefix of the operation\r\n          ID is enough.\r\n          \r\n          When loading the repo at an earlier operation, the working copy will be ignored, as if\r\n          `--ignore-working-copy` had been specified.\r\n          \r\n          It is possible to run mutating commands when loading the repo at an earlier operation.\r\n          Doing that is equivalent to having run concurrent commands starting at the earlier\r\n          operation. There's rarely a reason to do that, but it is possible.\r\n          \r\n          [aliases: --at-op]\r\n\r\n      --debug\r\n          Enable debug logging\r\n\r\n      --color <WHEN>\r\n          When to colorize output\r\n          \r\n          [possible values: always, never, debug, auto]\r\n\r\n      --quiet\r\n          Silence non-primary command output\r\n          \r\n          For example, `jj file list` will still list files, but it won't tell you if the working\r\n          copy was snapshotted or if descendants were rebased.\r\n          \r\n          Warnings and errors will still be printed.\r\n\r\n      --no-pager\r\n          Disable the pager\r\n\r\n      --config <NAME=VALUE>\r\n          Additional configuration options (can be repeated)\r\n          \r\n          The name should be specified as TOML dotted keys.
